coredata.dc:title: Carboxymethylcellulose/agar-based functional film incorporated with nitrogen-doped polyethylene glycol-derived carbon dots for active packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Tammina, Sai Kumar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: The present investigation demonstrates the role of nitrogen doping on polyethylene glycol (PEG)-derived carbon dots on optical, antibacterial, and antioxidant activity. CDs' average size and surface charge were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and a zeta sizer with 2.14 ± 0.6 nm and −20 mV, respectively. Though CDs without N-doping (PCD) did not show any significant antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, the CDs doped with nitrogen (NPCD) showed potent antioxidant (25% and 100% DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity) and significant antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive (1.8 cm inhibition zone) and Gram-negative (1.4 cm) bacteria. Both carbon dots were loaded into the carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)/agar-based film with different concentrations (4 and 8%) and showed a significant increase in the physicochemical properties, and UV-blocking property was increased from 53.7 to 79.9% without sacrificing the transparency. The NPCD-loaded film also showed high antioxidant (DPPH 12.7% and ABTS 67%) and potent antibacterial activity. In particular, the CMC/agar film loaded with 8% NPCD destroyed Escherichia coli and Listeria monocytogenes completely after 6 h of incubation.

head.ce:title: Carboxymethylcellulose/agar-based functional film incorporated with nitrogen-doped polyethylene glycol-derived carbon dots for active packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present investigation demonstrates the role of nitrogen doping on polyethylene glycol (PEG)-derived carbon dots on optical, antibacterial, and antioxidant activity. CDs' average size and surface charge were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and a zeta sizer with 2.14 ± 0.6 nm and −20 mV, respectively. Though CDs without N-doping (PCD) did not show any significant antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, the CDs doped with nitrogen (NPCD) showed potent antioxidant (25% and 100% DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity) and significant antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive (1.8 cm inhibition zone) and Gram-negative (1.4 cm) bacteria. Both carbon dots were loaded into the carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)/agar-based film with different concentrations (4 and 8%) and showed a significant increase in the physicochemical properties, and UV-blocking property was increased from 53.7 to 79.9% without sacrificing the transparency. The NPCD-loaded film also showed high antioxidant (DPPH 12.7% and ABTS 67%) and potent antibacterial activity. In particular, the CMC/agar film loaded with 8% NPCD destroyed Escherichia coli and Listeria monocytogenes completely after 6 h of incubation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the hectic modern lifestyle and the growing demand for fresh-cut fruits and vegetables, and prepared foods, there is a need to extend the shelf life of fresh and processed foods. To this end, food packaging plays an important role and helps to meet consumer needs. However, food ingredients are generally prone to deterioration over time, so safety and quality are always a concern, increasing the demand for suitable and safe packaging (Saravanakumar et al., 2020). The two most important factors responsible for food spoilage are microbial contamination and oxidation. Pathogenic contamination of common foodborne pathogens, such as Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes, and Aspergillus niger, etc. Can lead to fatal health problems (Sarengaowa et al., 2020). In addition, oxidation by oxygen or prolonged exposure to UV light can change the taste and quality of food (Roy et al., 2022). Therefore, new approaches are being utilized to maintain food quality and extend shelf life. However, in the case of packaged food, consumers complain that the expiration date is inadequate (Gaikwad et al., 2020; Giménez et al., 2012). Over the past decade, active packaging has been heavily researched and utilized to maintain food quality, safety, and extended shelf life (Kumar et al., 2018). A major solution to this is the addition of functional substances to packaging materials that provide antibacterial and antioxidant properties. These multifunctional nanofillers suppress microbial contamination and physicochemical changes in packaged food ingredients and ensure safety and quality (Roy and Rhim, 2020; Roy et al., 2021a). The incorporation of active chemicals into the base polymers of food packaging is now the most widely used technique for the production of active packaging (Priyadarshi and Rhim, 2020).

Although plastic materials are the most popular and widely used food packaging materials, biopolymers and bioplastics have recently attracted attention due to their biodegradability and sustainable properties. If plastic packaging is not disposed of properly after use, it will not decompose, which puts a great burden on the environment (Thompson et al., 2005). Ogunola et al. reported that in 2018, about 50% of plastic materials were emitted directly into the environment, weighing 300 million metric tons of non-degradable unsegregated waste (Ogunola et al., 2018). Discarded plastics are not easy to recycle because they are made of complex mixtures of various polymers. Therefore, it is necessary to develop non-toxic, biocompatible, biodegradable, and sustainable bioplastic materials that can replace non-degradable plastics (Mostafa et al., 2020). Most of these biopolymers are derived from biomass, and even if discarded after use, the ecosystem has no problem because they are naturally biodegradable. Among the various biopolymers, agar is one of the most interesting because it is thermoplastic, biodegradable, biocompatible, and offers good mechanical strength and reasonable water resistance (Rhim, 2012; Wu et al., 2009). Due to these characteristics, agar has been proposed as a substitute for petroleum-based plastic packaging materials (Letendre et al., 2002; Rhim et al., 2013), which is because agar has a higher ability to gel and is more resistant to high temperatures and low pH than other gelling systems (Rhim, 2013). In addition to agar, the cellulose derivative carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) also provides advantageous film-forming qualities. Inexpensive, renewable, hydrophilic, biodegradable, and non-toxic (Makwana et al., 2020).

Active packaging is often functionalized using functional nanomaterials, essential oils, and physiologically active natural plant extracts (Priyadarshi et al., 2021c). Carbon dots (CDs) are a promising new material that can be considered environmentally friendly and sustainable in food packaging applications (Ezati et al., 2022b). The CD has received great attention in the past few years as a functional nanofiller for food packaging due to its hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and facile synthesis strategy (Wang et al., 2017). In addition, its importance as an active packaging material is emphasized due to its outstanding functional properties, such as UV-blocking, antioxidant, and antibacterial activity recently revealed (Konwar et al., 2015; Li et al., 2018). The CDs should be made easily and inexpensively using environmentally friendly precursors to justify the rationale for sustainable food packaging. By the way, it is known that polyethylene glycol (PEG) can be used as a passivating agent to generate biocompatible carbon quantum dots with bright and tunable photoluminescence because it is non-toxic, non-immunogenic, and readily conjugates with other biomolecules through simple chemical reactions (Peng et al., 2020; Veronese and Pasut, 2005). CDs passivated using PEGs of various molecular weights have been reported (Mitra et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2009). In addition, CDs derived directly from the carbonization of PEG have also been reported in some studies. For example, CDs derived from PEG-4000 and PEG-6000 have been prepared, which exhibit low toxicity and promising biocompatibility and have been utilized for bioimaging applications (Fan et al., 2014). However, the synthesis of CD from PEG-10,000 precursors has not yet been investigated.

Therefore, we aimed for the first time to synthesize CDs using PEG-10,000 and investigated their conformational, structural, and optical properties. Also, the CDs were doped with nitrogen to improve their functional properties and compared to undoped CDs. In addition, this study is also the first report on using PEG-derived CD in the development of food packaging films. For this purpose, a CMC/Agar blend was used as a matrix, films were prepared using undoped and doped CDs, and the properties of the films were compared. Finally, the functional film's potential active food packaging applications were investigated by measuring its antioxidant and antibacterial activities.

PEG-10,000 was procured from Yakuri Pure Chemicals Co. Ltd. (Osaka, Japan). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was purchased from Daejung Chemicals and Metals Co. Ltd. (Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). TSB (Tryptic Soy Broth) and BHI (Brain Heart Infusion) media were brought from the Duksan Pure Chemicals Co. Ltd. (Ansan, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). Pathogenic bacterial strains, such as L. monocytogenes ATCC 15313 and E. coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895, were obtained from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC, Seoul, Korea). ABTS (2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid), DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl), and potassium persulfate were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA).

CDs were synthesized hydrothermally using polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a raw material. To this end, 50 mL of water was added to 1 g of polyethylene glycol and stirred for 30 min, and then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C. for 6 h to obtain PCD. After cooling the reaction mixture to room temperature, the final solution was prepared using Whatman No. 42 filter paper, and the filtrate was filtered again through a 0.2 μm membrane filter to remove unreacted substances or impurities from the CD solution (Fig. 1 ). In addition, N-doped PCD (NPCD) was prepared by adding 3 mL of ethylenediamine to the reaction mixture of the PCD formulation, and following the same procedure. The CD solutions were lyophilized and stored at 4 °C for further testing.

To determine the characteristic optical properties of CDs, the light absorption spectra of the CD solutions were recorded using a spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP series UV–Vis spectrophotometer, Seoul, Korea) in the range of 200–800 nm. Also, the fluorescence properties of the aqueous CD solutions were investigated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, F-7100, Japan). Morphological analysis was performed using a transmission electron microscope to evaluate the size and shape of the synthesized particles (HR-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Particle size distribution and average particle size were determined from the obtained images using ImageJ software. The chemical structure of the CD was determined using an FTIR spectrophotometer (ATR PRO 4X FTIR spectrophotometer, Jasco, Japan). In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were performed using a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Massachusetts, USA). A zeta potential analyzer was used to measure CDs' surface charge and stability in aqueous solutions (Zetasizer Nano ZS-ZEN 3600, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).

The antioxidant activity of CDs was determined using the DPPH and ABTS methods. For the DPPH assay, a DPPH solution was prepared by adding 4 mg of DPPH to 150 mL of methanol. To 10 mL DPPH solution, a varying amount of CDs (to make the final concentration 12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL) were added and incubated in the dark for 30 min. After this, the absorbance of the samples was recorded at 517 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. A test solution without CD was used as a control.

For the ABTS assay, an aqueous solution containing potassium persulfate (2.45 mM) and ABTS (7 mM) was prepared and incubated in the dark for 16 h. The absorbance of the mixture solution was set to 1 (a.u.) at 734 nm using distilled water. To 10 mL of the prepared solution, a varying amount of CDs (to make the final concentration 12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL) were added and incubated in the dark for 30 min before their absorbance values were recorded at 734 nm. The prepared solution without CDs was used as a control. The antioxidant activity of the CDs was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
A n t i o x i d a n t
a c t i v i t y
( % )
=
[
(
A o

A T
)
/
A o

× 100
where Ao and AT are the absorbances of both the control and test sample.

The antibacterial activity of the synthesized CDs against foodborne pathogenic bacteria, Escherichia coli and Listeria monocytogenes, was evaluated using a well-diffusion method. The fresh inoculum was prepared by taking 10 μL of bacterial strain in 20 mL of sterile peptone water and allowed to grow for 16 h under incubation at 37 °C and diluting the inoculum solution to obtain a final culture of 106–107 CFU/mL. After that, 100 μL of each bacterial inoculum was spread on agar plates with TSB and BHI media, respectively, and wells with a volume of 50 μL were punctured in the solidified agar media on the plates. 50 μL of PCD or NPCD solution was added to each well, the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 16 h, and the area of inhibition was measured in centimeters.

CMC/agar/CD films were prepared using a solution casting method (Roy and Rhim, 2021a). For this, 2 g of CMC, 2 g of agar, and 1.2 g of glycerol were dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water while mixing at 90 °C for 1 h. For preparing CD-added films, various amounts of PCD and NPCD (0, 4, and 8 wt% based on the polymer) were added to the film solution with vigorous mixing. Then, the film solution was cast onto a Teflon film-coated glass plate and dried for 48 h at room temperature (Fig. 1). The dried films were peeled off the plates and conditioned at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity (RH) in a humidity chamber for 72 h before further testing. The prepared films were designated as C/A, C/A/PCD4%, C/A/PCD8%, C/A/NPCD4%, and C/A/NPCD8%, respectively.

The morphology of the films was examined using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Matsuda, Japan). The functional groups in the films were identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Massachusetts, USA) and attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, ATR Pro-FTIR 4X-Jasco, Japan).

The films' color values were assessed using a chromameter (Konica Minolta CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) using a standard white color plate (L = 97.75, a = 0.49, and b = 1.96). The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated as follows:
(2)
Δ E =
(
Δ L
)
2
+
(
Δ a
)
2

Δ b
)
2
where ΔL, Δa, and Δb are the difference between each color value of the standard color plate and film sample.

Using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea), the films’ light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660) were measured to assess the UV-barrier and transparency of the films, respectively.

First, film strips (25 mm × 150 mm) were prepared using a precision double-blade cutter (Metrotec LB.02/A, Metrotec, SA, San Sebastian, Spain) to measure the film's mechanical properties. A digital micrometer with a precision of 1 μm (QuantuMike IP 65, Mitutoyo Corporation, Kanagawa, Japan) was used to measure the film thickness at five random spots on each strip, and the average thickness was determined. Following the ASTM A882-88 standard method, the mechanical characteristics of the films were assessed using an Instron universal testing machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA). The machine was run in the tensile mode at a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min with an initial grip gap of 50 mm and determined the film's percentage elongation at break (EB) and tensile strength (TS).

The water vapor permeability of the films was evaluated following the ASTM E96-95 standard method. The film sections (75 mm × 75 mm) were placed on the top of the gravimetric cup filled with 18 mL of distilled water (68 mm diameter with 25 mm depth) and sealed tightly to allow the passage of water vapor only through the film. The assembled WVP cups were placed in a humidity chamber (RHFX1077, Jeio Tech Co. Ltd., Ansan, South Korea) set at 25 °C and 50% RH. The difference in cup weight was noted for every hour for 8 h. The films' water vapor transmission rate (WVTR, g/m2. s) was determined from the slope of the weight change of the WVP cup vs. the time curve. The WVP (g.m/m2.Pa.s) was calculated as follows:
(3)
W V P =
(
W V T R × L
)
/ Δ p
where Δp and L are the difference in vapor pressure (Pa) across the film and film thickness (m), respectively.

The antioxidant activity of the film was evaluated using the DPPH and ABTS free radical scavenging methods. The DPPH and ABTS solutions were prepared as mentioned in previous sections. 50 mg of each film sample was added to 10 mL DPPH and ABTS solutions and incubated for 30 min in the dark before recording the absorbance at 517 and 734 nm, respectively, using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

The antimicrobial activity of the film against E. coli and L. monocytogenes was evaluated using the total viable colony count method. The bacterial broth cultures were prepared under sterile conditions using TSB and BHI media, respectively. The prepared cultures were incubated at 37 °C for 16 h. After incubation, the broths were diluted to 105–106 CFU/mL 20 mL of the broth solution was transferred to a 100 mL sterilized conical flask containing 100 mg of each film sample and incubated at 37 °C with mild shaking. The broth without film was used as a control. After 6 h of incubation, 50 μL culture from each flask was inoculated onto agar plates in triplicate. Each bacterial strain's total viable colony count was estimated as the percentage reduction in bacterial colonies.

The elemental composition of NPCD was determined using XPS to confirm the nitrogen doping of CD. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) shows the binding energies of carbon (70.24%), nitrogen (10.02%), and oxygen (16.73%) were identified at 285 eV, 400 eV, and 532 eV, respectively. Further, these elemental bands were deconvoluted to obtain bands corresponding to different states of each element. Fig. 2b shows the C 1s spectrum, which shows the binding energies at 284.8 eV and 286.1 eV confirming the existence of C–C/CC and C–N functional groups in the structure of carbon dots, and the peak observed at 287.5 eV represents the CO/CN functional moieties (Chen et al., 2016). The deconvolution of N 1s generated a band at 399 eV (Fig. 2c), which belongs to the binding energy of the C–N group, and another peak at 400.6 eV might be originated from N–H functional group (Ramanan et al., 2018). Also, the O 1s deconvolution (Fig. 2d) resulted in two different binding energy bands at 530 eV and 532.5 eV, illustrating the existence of C–O and CO oxygen bonding states, respectively (Xu et al., 2014). The results confirm that the surface of NPCD is functionalized with nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups, and their presence may be the reason for the high functional and biological activity of NPCD.

The size and morphology of the NPCD were evaluated using TEM analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. S1. The high-resolution TEM image of carbon dots (Fig. S1a) shows that most of the particles are almost spherical shaped within a size range of 1–4 nm without any aggregation. The average particle size of the CDs was 2.14 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. S2b).

Carbon dots' zeta (ζ) potential or electrokinetic potential was measured to determine the surface charge of carbon dots. Fig. S2c shows the zeta potential value of the NPCD, which is −20 mV. The high zeta potential of NCPD is probably due to negatively charged functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 on the surface of NPCD. The high zeta potential of NCPD can provide colloidal and electrostatic stability (Clogston and Patri, 2011; W. Li et al., 2017). On the other hand, PCD shows a zeta potential value of −9.55 mV, indicating relatively low stability compared to NPCD. Nitrogen doping imparted more negative charges to PCD, affecting the zeta potential of the CDs. Similarly, Issa et al. also reported an increasing electric potential of CD after doping with nitrogen (Issa et al., 2019).

The optical absorption and emission properties of carbon dots can be evaluated via UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry, which mainly reveal electronic transitions associated with a given electromagnetic radiation. The optical properties of PCD and NPCD are shown in Fig. 3 . There was a difference in the light absorption pattern of both CDs. The PCD absorbed the maximum energy at 278 nm, which might be due to the n-π* transitions from the CO group (Liu et al., 2017; Tammina et al., 2020), while the NPCD showed a shoulder band at 338 nm, which might be due to the n-π* transition and there was no 278 nm absorption band in the UV spectrum of NPCD (Fig. 3e). Also, the other bands at 255 and 244 nm were observed in the PCD and NPCD, respectively, due to the aromatic structures of carbon dots from the π-π* transitions (Liu et al., 2017; Tammina et al., 2020). The change in the maximum absorption wavelength of the two CDs may be due to the change in the energy level of the carbon point before and after doping with a nitrogen source, where the unbound electrons of nitrogen may play an important role. Emission data showed that PCD exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior with a regular decrease in intensity with increasing excitation energy (Fig. 3a), and maximum emission was observed at 367 nm with an excitation energy of 300 nm (Fig. 3b). However, the maximum emission of NPCD was different (Fig. 3d). Although NPCD showed excitation-dependent behavior starting at 300 nm wavelength, after 330 nm excitation, the maximum emission shifted from 370 nm to 425 nm and then to 442 nm as the excitation energy increased (Fig. 3c). The maximum emission intensity of NPCD was observed at 442 nm with an excitation energy of 366 nm (Fig. 3d). This observation shows that the modification of the surface functional groups leads to a change in the electron energy level of the carbon point followed by a change in the emission and absorption properties. Interestingly, the maximum emission intensity of PCD did not exceed 366 nm and at the same time, the maximum absorption of NPCD was found at 363 nm excitation energy (Fig. 3b,d). This is a remarkable characteristic of carbon dots due to surface changes. Nitrogen doping creates an intermediate bandgap energy state, resulting in a redshift of the bandgap shrinkage and emission due to the introduction of intermediate n orbitals among graphitic π and π* orbitals. (Do et al., 2016).

Table 1 compares the characteristics of selective existing literature and the current study, which shows that the molecular weight of the PEG influenced the optical properties of the carbon dots. The PCD showed an emission maximum at 360 nm, but all other carbon points derived from PEG of different molecular weights showed emission maximums within the 410–700 nm wavelength. Doping PCD with nitrogen shifted the maximum emission to red. Changes in surface passivation were also reflected in luminescence color, with PCD exhibiting blue luminescence (Fig. 3b) and NPCD exhibiting cyan or cyan emission (Fig. 3d). Moreover, the quantum yield of both PCD (10%) and NPCD (8%) was promising, and but doping influenced the QY efficiency of NPCD. However, doping can affect photobleaching, biological properties, and sensitivity for pathogens and analytes.

Antioxidant or radical scavenging activity shows any substance's radical scavenging or radical inhibition potential in the presence of a radical-generating agent. The antioxidant activity of CDs determined by the DPPH and ABTS methods are shown in Fig. 4 . The NPCD showed DPPH radical scavenging activity of about 25% at the maximum concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 4a). In contrast, the PCD did not show significant activity. On the other hand, for ABTS, PCD showed 20% scavenging activity, and NPCD showed 100% scavenging activity (Fig. 4b). The difference in CD scavenging potential for the two oxidizers may be due to the solvent. Because CD has hydrophilic properties, the release or activity of CD in polar solvents is greater than in less polar solvents. Therefore, for CD, the ABTS method using an aqueous solution shows higher antioxidant activity than the DPPH method using a methanol solvent (Nawaz et al., 2020). NPCD also exhibited higher antioxidant activity in the ABTS and DPPH methods, possibly due to surface functionalization with nitrogen. Although PCD exhibited little activity against ABTS and was completely inactivated against DPPH, this observation demonstrates that solvent polarity and nitrogen functionalization played an important role in radical scavenging activity. At the same time, the electron-donating group of the carbon point and the structure of the carbon sp2 domain are the main causes of this phenomenon. (Y. Li et al., 2017; Qiu et al., 2014). These two factors promote free radical destruction and delocalization of electron spins in the core carbon structure (Ruiz et al., 2017). The presence of functional groups can promote the radical scavenging activity of CDs, mainly derived from OH, NH2, CO, and C–N groups. This group is involved in reducing radicals through either proton donation or adduct formation after electron donation with delocalization of free electrons (Fig. 4c), which further leads to the stabilization of the sp2 hybridized carbon network (Das et al., 2021; Ilyasov et al., 2020).

Antibacterial activity reveals the CD's ability towards bacterial pathogens. Agar well-diffusion method was used to evaluate its potential by measuring the inhibition zone against E. coli and L. monocytogenes. NPCD showed an inhibition zone of up to 1.4 cm for E. coli and 1.8 cm for L. monocytogenes, however, PCD showed no antimicrobial activity against both pathogens (Fig. 4). These results indicate that nitrogen doping affected the antimicrobial potential of carbon dots. The antibacterial activity of NPCD is presumed to be due to functional groups such as amides and amines on the surface. Protons on the amine/amide group promote the penetration of CD into the negatively charged microbial cell wall. In addition, the inhibition zone was larger in Listeria monocytogenes than in E. coli, possibly because the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria has a higher negative charge than the Gram-negative cell wall. The cell wall structure of Gram-positive bacteria has a higher amount of negatively charged teichoic acid or lipoteichoic acid residues. Consequently, it interacts better with protonated NPCD, resulting in higher antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes than E. coli (Ezati et al., 2022a).

The addition of carbon dots causes significant structural and morphological changes to the film. The XPS analysis results (Fig. 5 ) show the elements in the C/A/NPCD8% film. The survey spectrum shows the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s binding energies at 287.5 eV, 402 eV, and 534 eV, respectively (Fig. 5a). The deconvolution of C 1s indicated the presence of functional groups of C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO/CN designated by binding energies 284.7 eV, 286.19 eV, and 287.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b). Also, the functional groups related to the N 1s were observed at binding energies of 398.9 eV and 410.4 eV, which were attributed to the C–N and N–H groups, respectively (Fig. 5c) (Sadhanala et al., 2021). Further, the deconvolution of the O 1s narrow band displayed binding energies of CO and C–O at 531.1 eV and 532.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 5d) (Zhuo et al., 2018). These results confirm the existence of NPCD functional groups in the film.

The FESEM was used to evaluate the influence of carbon dots on the homogeneity and smoothness of the film before and after the incorporation of CDs. The surface and cross-sectional images of the neat C/A and carbon dots-incorporated C/A films are shown in Fig. S2, where all films displayed an intact morphology free of voids and cracks in surface SEM pictures. The neat C/A film exhibited a smooth surface indicating good compatibility between CMC and agar. The cross-sectional image exhibits a slightly wrinkled morphology characteristic of the C/A film, as previously reported (Roy et al., 2021b). A concentration-dependent change in morphology was observed in the PCD-added films. Surface images showed some undulation with increasing PCD concentration. However, the furrows in the cross-sectional image were reduced, and the morphology was smoother, indicating improved compatibility between PCD and polymer chains. A possible reason for this could be the H-bond interaction between the hydroxyl functional groups on the PCD surface and the functional groups on the polymer chain (Priyadarshi et al., 2021a). However, no similar change in morphology was observed with increasing concentration of NPCD in the C/A matrix. Nevertheless, the two carbon dots were uniformly dispersed throughout the polymer matrix without agglomeration. All films exhibit a dense structure that exhibits good compatibility and miscibility between the polymer and the carbon dots.

The characteristic peaks of the neat C/A film and the changes after loading CDs in the mid-infrared region were evaluated using an FT-IR spectrophotometer. Here, functional groups of CMC and agar in the control film were also found in all CD-loaded films (Fig. 6 f.). Stretching oscillations at 3263 cm−1 occurred at the hydroxyl (O–H) group due to hydrogen bonding interactions. Two transmittance bands at 2925 cm−1 and 2877 cm−1 corresponded to the polymer's C–H stretching vibrations from the methyl group (Roy and Rhim, 2021b; Vukic et al., 2020). The characteristic vibration bands at 1714 cm−1, 1413 cm−1, and 1035 cm−1 correspond to the CO, aromatic CC, and alcoholic C–O groups, respectively (Vukic et al., 2020). The other vibrational bands at 2925 cm−1 and 2877 cm−1 are related to the alkyl groups (C–H) of polymer composite. The transmittance bands at 1590 cm−1 and 1413 cm−1 were attributed to the carboxyl groups of CMC from asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively (El Miri et al., 2015). Also, the bands related to the ester sulfate groups of agar and an aliphatic group of cellulose were found at 1374 cm−1 and 1320 cm−1, respectively (Roy and Rhim, 2019; Soni et al., 2015). Besides, the ester-sulfate bond and CO group from β-galactose agar were observed at 1035 cm−1 and 931 cm−1, respectively (Roy and Rhim, 2019). All films showed similar patterns in the FTIR absorption spectra except for the difference in strength, indicating that the chemical structure of the polymer composite film did not change significantly even after adding carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color and optical properties of films
ce:section.ce:para: The transparency of the C/A and CD-added C/A films is shown in Fig. 6a–e. The neat C/A film was highly transparent without any color tint. The appearance of the film did not change significantly when PCD was added, but the film became slightly yellowish when NCPD was added. The color and optical characteristics of the C/A and CD-added C/A films are shown in Table 2 . The lightness (L-value) of the C/A film did not change significantly (p > 0.05) by adding PCD and NPCD, except for the 8% addition of NCPD. However, the greenness (a-value) of the film decreased significantly (p < 0.05) by adding PCD and NPCD, while the yellowness (b-value) of the film increased significantly (p < 0.05), and the change in a- and b-values were more significant when NCPD was added. The film's total color difference (ΔE) did not change significantly when PCD was added but increased significantly when NCPD was added. As a result, the NPCD-added film was lower in brightness and higher in greenness, yellowness, and total color difference than the PCD-added film, which is mainly due to that NPCD having a nitrogen functional group showed a darker brown color than that of PCD.

UV protection is an important property of functional food packaging film to prevent food spoilage because food generally deteriorates when exposed to UV light (Lin et al., 2022). Fig. 6g shows the light transmission spectra of the C/A-based films. The neat C/A film is transparent and exhibits higher light transmittance in the UV and visible range. When PCD and NPCD were added, the transmittance of light decreased according to the concentration of CD, and the decrease in light transmittance was more pronounced in the UV region (<400 nm) than in the visible region. The transparency and UV-barrier properties of the film were evaluated by determining the light transmittance at 660 nm (T660) and 280 nm (T280), respectively, and the results are shown in Table 2. The T660 of the film was slightly reduced with the addition of the CDs. However, T280 decreased more significantly, indicating that the addition of the CDs increased the UV-blocking properties of the film with little sacrificing transparency. The NPCD-added film exhibited higher UV-blocking properties than the PCD-added film, which may be due to the presence of amino groups on the NPCD surface to promote n-π* and π-π* transitions (Fig. 2).

The mechanical properties of food packaging films are very important because the main purpose of the packaging material is to protect food from mechanical damage (Sangroniz et al., 2019). Table 3 shows the mechanical properties of the C/A-based films. The thickness of the C/A film was not significantly changed with the addition of the CDs except for the 8 wt% addition of NCPD (C/A/NCPD8% film). The tensile strength of the C/A film decreased with the addition of CD according to the concentration, and the decrease of TS was more significant with the addition of NCPD than with CPD. In contrast, the flexibility of the film (EB) increased with the addition of the CDs. A probable reason for this may be the higher repulsion within the NPCD possessing a high negative surface charge, as confirmed by the zeta potential results. As the amount of NPCD in the film increases, the repulsive force between particles increases, and the intermolecular interaction decreases, resulting in a decrease in tensile strength. The flexibility of the film (EB) increased with the addition of CDs, which could be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the CDs, which can act as a plasticizer.

Since limiting or reducing the movement of moisture between food and the environment is one of the primary purposes of food packaging, a packaging film's water vapor permeability (WVP) should be as low as possible. The film's water vapor barrier and surface hydrophilicity were evaluated by measuring the WVP and WCA of the film, and the results are shown in Table 3. The WVP of the C/A film did not change significantly (p > 0.05) with the addition of CPD and NCPD. However, the WCA of the film significantly decreased with the addition of the CDs, depending on the type of CD and concentration. The WCA of the NCPD-added film was more significantly reduced compared to the CPD-added film, which may be due to the higher hydrophilicity of NCPD than CPD.

The antioxidant activity of the C/A-based films determined by the DPPH and ABTS methods is shown in Fig. 7 a. The control (C/A film) and the PCD-added films did not show any antioxidant activity in the DPPH method. In contrast, the NPCD-added films showed significant DPPH scavenging activity, where the C/A/NPCD4% film showed 2.8%, and the C/A/NPCD8% film showed 12.7% antioxidant activity. In the ABTS method, the neat C/A and PCD-added (C/A/PCD4% and C/A/PCD8%) films showed significant antioxidant activity of 20%, 24%, and 67%, respectively. In contrast, the NPCD-added films exhibited more potent antioxidant activity of 100% scavenging of ABTS free radicals. Here, the antioxidant activity in the neat C/A film (20% of ABTS activity) can be due to the reaction of exposed electron-donating hydroxyl groups in CMC with ABTS free radicals over time (Priyadarshi et al., 2021b). The low antioxidant activity of the film in the DPPH method is due to the limited release of the active ingredient (i.e., CDs) into a nonpolar methanol solution in the DPPH method (Nawaz et al., 2020).

Prevention of food contamination by bacteria is one of the most serious issues in securing food safety. Packaging materials with antibacterial properties can extend the shelf life of food by minimizing microbial problems. The antimicrobial activity of the C/A-based films was evaluated against E. coli and L. monocytogenes, and the results are shown in Fig. 7b. The neat C/A and PCD-added films showed no bactericidal effect against E. coli and L. monocytogenes. However, the NPCD-added films (C/A/NCPD4% and C/A/NCPD8%) showed significant bactericidal activity of 70 and 100% antibacterial activity against E. coli and 77.3 and 100% against L. monocytogenes. The NPCD-added films exhibit significant antimicrobial activity compared to the control and PCD-added films, which agrees with the antimicrobial activity of PCD and NPCD assessed using the well-diffusion method (Fig. 4 d,e).

The reasons for the antibacterial action of the film are as follows. When a CD-loaded film is added to an aqueous medium composed of nutrient solution and bacteria, the CD is released from the film into an aqueous solution/broth. As previously described (section 3.4), among PCD and NPCD, only NPCD showed an inhibition zone of bacteria due to the nitrogen-rich portion of its surface. (Fig. 4 d,e). The unique electron-donating nature of nitrogen moieties promotes the production of reactive oxygen species/superoxide ions (O2 −) (Stöhr et al., 1991; Strelko et al., 2000, 2004). In addition, the possibility of forming reactive oxygen species on the CD surface by electrostatic force may remain on the surface (Strelko et al., 2004). The oxygen radical species thus formed can further promote the formation of OH− when CDs are in an aqueous environment, and an aquatic environment can support this. At the same time, the existing OH− and O2 − can further sustain the HO2 − formation due to the reductive property of the functional moieties (Strelko et al., 2004). The electron-donating properties of amides/amines result from their antioxidant production. These antioxidants may affect their antibacterial properties against pathogens (Travlou et al., 2018). These antioxidants are known to impair cell physiology, including lipids, metabolites, nucleotides, and proteins, leading to death (Dwyer et al., 2014) (Fig. 7c). The strong antimicrobial properties of NPCD-loaded film against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria allow them to be used in active food packaging applications.

Carbon dots were prepared using PEG as an environmentally friendly and sustainable source and doped with nitrogen to improve their functional properties. N-doped CDs showed distinct morphological, chemical, and functional differences from undoped CDs, and N-doping significantly increased the antioxidant and antibacterial properties of the CDs. The addition of CDs, especially N-doped CDs, to the C/A-based films, reduced the films' mechanical strength and surface hydrophobicity but maintained the same level of water vapor barrier and improved UV blocking, antioxidant, and antibacterial activities. Nitrogen doping played an important role in tuning the properties of CDs and film properties, especially the free radical scavenging, bactericidal, and UV blocking properties of the films were significantly improved. The N-doped CD, which has excellent antibacterial and antioxidant activity, is expected to be used in the development of sustainable multifunctional food packaging materials by being added to food packaging films as a functional material.

Sai Kumar Tammina: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation and analysis, Writing – original draft, Validation. Jong-Whan Rhim: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
